title: OLED vs QLED vs Mini-LED vs MicroLED: Which TV Tech Is Right for You?
excerpt: TV shopping is confusing. We break down every display technology in plain English so you can make the right choice.
content:
## The Problem With TV Shopping

Walk into any electronics store and you'll be bombarded with acronyms — OLED, QLED, Mini-LED, MicroLED, QNED, Crystal UHD. It's overwhelming. This guide cuts through the noise and tells you exactly what each technology means and who it's best for.

---

## OLED — The King of Contrast

OLED (Organic Light-Emitting Diode) is the gold standard for picture quality. Every single pixel produces its own light and can turn off completely — which means **true black**. There's no light bleeding from a backlight. The result? Infinite contrast and colours that pop in a way no other technology can match.

**Key specs:** ~1,000–2,000 nits peak brightness | Infinite contrast ratio | Wide viewing angles

**Best for:** Dark room viewers, cinephiles, premium buyers who want the best possible picture

**Watch out for:** Burn-in risk if you leave static images on screen for long periods (though modern OLEDs have built-in protections). Not the brightest in daylight.

---

## QLED — The Bright Room Champion

QLED (Quantum Light-Emitting Diode) is Samsung's branding for LED TVs that use quantum dot technology. They're still LED-backlit screens, but the quantum dots produce more vibrant, accurate colours. The big win? **Raw brightness** — up to 4,000 nits on flagship models.

**Key specs:** Up to 4,000 nits peak brightness | Good (not infinite) contrast | No burn-in risk

**Best for:** Bright living rooms, sports fans, gamers, families

**Watch out for:** Blacks aren't as deep as OLED — you'll see slight grey in dark scenes.

---

## Mini-LED — The Best of Both Worlds

Mini-LED is an evolution of traditional LED backlighting. Instead of a handful of large LEDs behind the screen, you get thousands of tiny LEDs organised into dimming zones. This gives much better local dimming — darker blacks than standard QLED, while keeping the brightness advantage of LED.

**Key specs:** 1,000–3,000 nits | Very good contrast | No burn-in risk

**Best for:** Buyers who want near-OLED quality without the burn-in risk, bright and mixed-light rooms

**Watch out for:** Blooming (a halo effect around bright objects on dark backgrounds) can still occur on cheaper models.

---

## MicroLED / MicroRGB — The Future (Now)

MicroLED is the next frontier. Each pixel is its own microscopic LED — no organic compounds, no backlight. You get OLED-level blacks AND LED-level brightness. Samsung's MicroRGB goes further, splitting each pixel into separate R, G, B LEDs for extraordinary colour accuracy.

**Key specs:** 3,000–10,000+ nits | Infinite contrast | No burn-in risk | 30-year lifespan

**Best for:** Premium buyers who want the absolute best — no compromises

**Watch out for:** Very expensive. Still relatively new technology.

---

## Quick Comparison

| Technology | Blacks | Brightness | Burn-in Risk | Price Range | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OLED | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ | Low | £800–£3,000+ | Dark rooms, cinema |
| QLED | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★★ | None | £400–£2,500+ | Bright rooms, sports |
| Mini-LED | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★☆ | None | £600–£2,000+ | Mixed lighting |
| MicroLED | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | None | £3,000+ | Premium, no compromise |

---

## So Which Should You Buy?

- **Dark room + love cinema?** → OLED
- **Bright room + sports/gaming?** → QLED or Mini-LED
- **Want the best of both worlds?** → Mini-LED
- **Budget is no object?** → MicroLED / MicroRGB

Still not sure? Let us figure it out for you.
